source: FII Institute — Rise of the Gigafirm: next trillion-dollar tech company (panel)
summary: Foundation model companies attached to giant internet platforms with proprietary data will be immensely valuable; only a few qualify
summary_extended: Baker argues that the most valuable AI companies of the next decade will be foundation model companies that are tightly coupled to giant internet platforms with proprietary, hard-to-replicate data moats — and that only a tiny handful of companies qualify for this structural advantage. He specifically names Google (Gemini attached to Search, YouTube, Gmail, Maps) and Microsoft/OpenAI (GPT models attached to Office 365, Azure, LinkedIn, Bing) as the two clearest examples of this archetype, and also cites Elon Musk's xAI/X (Grok attached to the X social graph and real-time data) as a third candidate. Meta, despite its scale and data assets, has disqualified itself from this value-capture dynamic by choosing to open-source its foundation models (LLaMA), effectively commoditizing its own model layer. The investment implication is highly concentrated: Baker views this as a structural long on GOOGL and MSFT specifically, with the universe of qualifying public equities being extremely small, and suggests investors should not expect many new entrants to this category.
detail: Baker identifies Google, Microsoft/OpenAI, and xAI/X as the qualifying platform-attached foundation model companies. Meta has chosen open-source, removing itself from this value capture. The set is extremely small.
confidence_evidence: Baker names specific companies and frames this as a high-confidence structural view
quote_fragment: foundation model company attached to a giant internet platform with proprietary data

ticker_audit.reasoning: Google is explicitly named as a primary qualifying platform-attached foundation model company, with Gemini tied to Search, YouTube, Gmail, and Maps as proprietary data moats.
Microsoft/OpenAI is explicitly named as a qualifying platform-attached foundation model company, with GPT models tied to Office 365, Azure, LinkedIn, and Bing.

Baker explicitly names Meta as a company that has removed itself from this value-capture dynamic by open-sourcing LLaMA, making it a direct subject of the thesis as a negative case or excluded company — clearly implied and central to the argument.
